He noted that in countries with democratic principles, “leaders who lose in party elections should make way for the victors.” His final act as minister was completing the 13th Malaysia Plan.

Anwar reportedly urged Rafizi to reconsider. He refused.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

Rafizi was the most credible reformist in Anwar’s Cabinet. He designed PADU, pushed subsidy rationalisation, championed data-driven governance, and was the intellectual engine behind PH’s economic reform agenda. His departure left a vacuum at exactly the moment Malaysia needed steady economic stewardship.

Bloomberg called it “a blow to Anwar’s government.” The Straits Times noted his exit “will leave a gap at a time when Malaysia’s economic reforms are stalling.” CNA reported that the party election was “marred by allegations of vote-rigging and nepotism.”

Two ministers quitting over a party election isn’t normal. It’s a signal that something is deeply wrong inside PKR.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

Anwar lost his best minister because he allowed — or enabled — his daughter to challenge and destroy the incumbent. Rafizi didn’t lose because he was incompetent. He lost because the party machinery was mobilised for the boss’s daughter.

The fact that Anwar then asked Rafizi to stay is the most cynical part. You orchestrate a humiliation, then ask the humiliated to keep working for you. That’s not leadership — that’s gaslighting.

Since Rafizi’s departure, he has become an increasingly vocal critic. He warned Anwar not to “underestimate public sentiment.” He called for MACC chief Azam Baki’s suspension. He’s now under MACC investigation himself — a timing that critics call retaliatory.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

Malaysian politics has a long tradition of purging internal rivals. Mahathir sidelined Anwar in 1998. Now Anwar has effectively sidelined Rafizi in 2025. The method is different — party elections instead of sodomy charges — but the outcome is the same: the leader’s most capable rival is removed.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

You lost your best economic mind because family politics mattered more than national interest. Rafizi built your policy credibility. He was the reason investors took PH seriously. And you let him walk — or pushed him out. The 13th Malaysia Plan was his last gift to you. What have you done with it since?

Sources: Straits Times | Bloomberg | FMT | CNA

item.title: Nurul Izzah Wins PKR Deputy President — Anwar Says It's Democracy, Not Nepotism
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister & PKR President

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-05-24

From Adviser to Deputy President: The Anwar Dynasty Takes Shape

In May 2025, Nurul Izzah Anwar was elected PKR Deputy President — the number two position in the party led by her father, the sitting Prime Minister. Anwar Ibrahim dismissed all criticism with a wave of the hand: she was chosen by members. Case closed. Except it isn’t.

What Happened

At the PKR National Congress in Johor Bahru on 24 May 2025, Nurul Izzah won the contest for Deputy President. The presidency — held by Anwar — was not up for contest. This means the top two positions in PKR are now held by father and daughter.

Anwar’s response was direct:

People will say that my wife and daughter are in the party. That may be true, but when we were fighting in 1998, there wasn’t even a congress.

The Problem With “Chosen by Members”

Yes, party members voted. But context matters:

  • Anwar is the party president. His endorsement — even if unstated — carries enormous weight in internal elections. PKR members know that voting against the president’s daughter carries political risk.
  • Patronage flows downward. In a system where the PM controls government appointments, contracts, and GLC positions, party members who align with the leadership family are better positioned. This is structural pressure, not free choice.
  • Former aide Ezam Nor went viral explaining that this was less about democracy and more about Anwar consolidating power ahead of anticipated political and legal challenges.

The Dynasty Tracker

  • Dr Wan Azizah Wan Ismail (Anwar’s wife): former PKR President, former Deputy PM
  • Nurul Izzah (daughter): former MP, Senior Economic Adviser to PM, now PKR Deputy President
  • The Anwar family has held or currently holds the top two party positions and a senior government advisory role Fact: No other political family in Malaysia’s reformist movement holds this concentration of power within a single party.

The Steelman (and Why It Fails)

Nurul Izzah has been in politics since her twenties, survived prison visits, campaigned during her father’s incarceration, and has policy credentials. She is not unqualified. But the question is not about qualifications — it is about whether a reformist party should have a father-daughter leadership structure while the father is also Prime Minister. In any mature democracy, this would be considered a governance red flag.

What Should Happen

  1. PKR should adopt rules preventing immediate family members of the president from holding the deputy presidency simultaneously.
  2. Anwar should have publicly stayed neutral in the contest.
  3. Malaysian civil society should stop accepting “the members chose” as a complete answer when the chooser’s father controls the party and the government.

Verdict: The reformasi movement was born out of opposition to Mahathir’s concentration of power. Three decades later, a new concentration is forming — this time with a family crest on it.

*Sources: Malay Mail · Free Malaysia Today · MalaysiaNow · *SCMP

item.title: Nurul Izzah Defeats Rafizi by 6,000 Votes for PKR Deputy President — Daddy's Party, Daddy's Rules
item.description:
Said by: PKR Party Elections / Nurul Izzah Anwar

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-05-23

📌 WHAT HAPPENED

On 23 May 2025, Nurul Izzah Anwar — the eldest daughter of Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim — defeated incumbent deputy president Rafizi Ramli in PKR’s internal elections by a commanding margin: 9,803 votes to 3,866.

The result was never in doubt. Over 200 of PKR’s 222 divisions had declared support for Nurul Izzah before the vote even took place. The election was held in Johor Bahru, with party president Anwar Ibrahim retaining the top post unchallenged.

Days later, Rafizi resigned as Economy Minister, saying he “no longer possesses the party’s mandate.” His ally, Natural Resources Minister Nik Nazmi, also quit.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

The PM’s daughter is now the second-most powerful person in the party that runs Malaysia. This isn’t just a party election — it’s a dynastic consolidation.

Anwar Ibrahim spent 25 years preaching reformasi. He promised to break the cycle of patronage, nepotism, and elite capture that defined Malaysian politics. Now his daughter holds the No. 2 party position, his wife is the president of PKR Wanita, and his political opponents within the party have been systematically sidelined.

The SCMP reported it as a “nepotism row.” Bloomberg called it a “blow to Anwar’s government.” Only in PKR would anyone pretend this was a normal democratic contest.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

Let’s be clear about what happened: the PM’s daughter ran against his own Economy Minister for control of the party, and the party apparatus delivered a landslide for the boss’s kid. Over 200 divisions declared for Nurul Izzah before a single ballot was cast. That’s not democracy — that’s a coronation.

Rafizi — the man who built PKR’s policy credibility, who designed PADU, who pushed subsidy rationalisation — was rewarded with humiliation. The message to every reformist in PKR is unmistakable: loyalty to the family trumps competence.

Nurul Izzah was even offered the chance to create a “second deputy president” post for Rafizi. She didn’t deny it. The consolation prize for losing to the boss’s daughter is a made-up title. That’s not a political party — it’s a family firm.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

Anwar already appointed Nurul Izzah as Senior Economic Adviser in January 2023, despite her losing her parliamentary seat. He dismissed nepotism accusations by redefining the word. Now the pattern escalates: from unelected adviser to party deputy president. Next stop: Deputy PM?

Malaysia has a long history of political dynasties — Mahathir groomed his son Mukhriz, Najib inherited his father’s legacy. Anwar promised to be different. He isn’t.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

You told us reformasi was about breaking the old ways. About meritocracy. About ending the politics of patronage and family. Your daughter now controls your party’s No. 2 slot with a 6,000-vote margin that was engineered before the ballots were printed. Your Economy Minister quit in protest. Your reform agenda lost its most credible champion. And you called it democracy. The only thing reformed in PKR is the family’s grip on power.

Sources: Straits Times | Malay Mail | SCMP | Bloomberg

item.title: Steven Sim's 'Adviser' Turns Out to Be Alleged RM10 Million Fraudster — Minister Claims He Never Appointed Anyone
item.description:
Said by: Steven Sim, Human Resources Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-03-31

📌 WHAT HAPPENED

In March 2025, a businessman with the title “Tan Sri” was arrested by Bukit Aman’s anti-money laundering team for allegedly defrauding the public of RM10 million. The 59-year-old, who had 30 years of experience in the oil and gas industry, was apprehended upon returning to his home at Jalan U Thant, KL, after a trip to Osaka, Japan.

A news portal then reported that the arrested businessman had been claiming to serve as an adviser to Human Resources Minister Steven Sim. Sim’s press secretary Abdul Hakim Ab Rahman quickly issued a denial, stating that the minister “has never appointed any individual as his advisor.”

The ministry directed all inquiries about positions within the minister’s office to “official channels” but offered no explanation for how an alleged fraudster came to be publicly associated with the minister.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS

This is a governance failure regardless of whether Sim actually appointed the man or not. If the man was falsely claiming to be the minister’s adviser, it raises questions about why Sim’s office didn’t know about it sooner and why action wasn’t taken before the man was arrested for a completely separate RM10 million fraud.

If the man did have some informal relationship with the minister — even without a formal appointment letter — then Sim’s blanket denial is misleading. In Malaysian politics, “advisers” often operate in grey zones without official documentation, precisely to maintain plausible deniability.

Either way, the fact that an alleged RM10 million fraudster was running around using a PH minister’s name to build credibility is a serious vetting and governance failure.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT

The speed of the denial is telling. Within hours of the news breaking, Sim’s office issued a statement saying the minister “has never appointed any individual as his advisor.” No investigation. No explanation. Just a flat denial.

But here’s what the denial doesn’t address: How did a man alleged to have defrauded the public of RM10 million come to be publicly associated with a sitting Cabinet minister? Was there any social contact? Any events attended together? Any photos? The denial addresses the formal appointment question but dodges the substantive question of the relationship.

PH ministers love to talk about transparency and accountability. Here’s a test: release a full accounting of everyone who has access to or works with the minister’s office, formally or informally. Until then, the denial is just PR crisis management.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES

Malaysia has a long tradition of politically connected fixers, “advisers,” and intermediaries who use their proximity to power to build business credibility. The Nurul Izzah “adviser” appointment controversy was a similar case of a PH figure being linked to questionable appointments. Under BN, the practice was endemic — “political secretaries” and “special officers” operated as unofficial gatekeepers with enormous informal power.

PH promised to clean this up. The fact that alleged fraudsters are still claiming ministerial connections suggests the cleanup hasn’t happened.

🎯 PH CALLOUT

You promised Kerajaan Madani would be different — no more cronyism, no more fixers, no more politically connected businessmen using ministerial names for personal gain. Then an alleged RM10 million fraudster turns out to be publicly claiming he’s your minister’s adviser, and the best you can do is a press release saying “we never appointed anyone.” That’s not accountability — that’s damage control. If you can’t even control who’s using your ministers’ names, how are you controlling who’s using their influence?

Sources: Malay Mail | The Vibes | Bernama | FMT

item.title: Anwar Hits Back at Critics Who Say He Travels Too Much — 'They Don't Understand Hard Work'
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-02-27

The Jet-Setting PM Who Can’t Take Criticism

When Malaysians questioned why their PM was constantly abroad while domestic crises mounted, Anwar’s response was not empathy — it was indignation. He hit out at critics, saying they were “clearly unaware of the hard work” that goes into overseas trips.

The Facts

Fact: Anwar defended his extensive overseas travel schedule, dismissing critics as uninformed.

Fact: In November 2024, his own disclosure that private companies funded 75% of travel costs raised further questions.

Fact: While the PM travels, Malaysians face: rising costs, healthcare crisis, education decline, housing unaffordability, and democratic backsliding.

Why It Matters

  • Every PM travels. The question is whether the results justify the frequency.
  • Dismissing legitimate questions about travel priorities as ignorance is the same arrogance PH promised to end.
  • Malaysians don’t begrudge diplomatic work. They begrudge a PM who lectures them about hard work while they can’t afford groceries. Sources: Multiple news reports

item.title: East Asia Forum: 'Reform Aspirations Clashed with Political Realities in 2024'
item.description:
Said by: Kai Ostwald, University of British Columbia

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-02-27

Academic analysis: ‘Anwar pivoted from political survival in 2023 to pursuing an ambitious reform agenda in 2024, with notable gains in economic growth. Yet political turbulence — driven by identity-based populism and an unwieldy coalition — continued to constrain domestic reforms.’ Sources: https://eastasiaforum.org/2025/02/27/malaysias-reform-aspirations-clashed-with-political-realities-in-2024/

item.title: J-Kom Propaganda Unit Lives On — PH Keeps the Machine It Once Condemned
item.description:
Said by: Fahmi Fadzil, Communications Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-02-06

PH dissolved JASA in 2018 calling it BN’s propaganda tool. Revived as J-Kom with RM85.5M budget. MP Hassan Abdul Karim called it ‘redundant’ and a ‘propaganda department for the ruling party.’

item.title: Najib Gets House Arrest — The World's Most Comfortable 'Punishment' for the World's Biggest Kleptocracy
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim Administration / Pardons Board

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-02-02

📌 What Happened

Former PM Najib Razak, convicted of corruption in the 1MDB scandal that looted billions from the Malaysian public, had his sentence halved by a royal pardon and was subsequently transferred to house arrest at his luxurious Kuala Lumpur residence. The man convicted of stealing from the rakyat now serves ’time’ in a mansion.

🔥 Why It Matters

1MDB was the largest kleptocracy case in history. US DOJ estimated $4.5 billion was misappropriated. Malaysia is STILL paying the debt — RM42 billion paid so far, RM50 billion total burden. Ordinary Malaysians bear this cost through reduced public services, higher taxes, and fiscal constraints. The man responsible lives in comfort while his victims pay his debts.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

Anwar spent his entire political career criticising Najib and 1MDB. He used 1MDB as his primary argument for why Malaysia needed change. He asked Malaysians to vote PH specifically to ensure accountability for 1MDB. Then, once in power, he allowed — or facilitated — Najib’s sentence reduction and comfortable house arrest. The anti-corruption crusader became the corruption enabler. The reason? UMNO’s parliamentary support requires keeping UMNO’s patron comfortable.

📜 Previous Cases

In 2018, when PH first won, Najib was arrested and charged. The legal process worked. Under PH 2.0, the legal process was undermined by political calculation. Zahid got 47 charges dropped. Najib got his sentence halved. The pattern is clear: PH trades justice for UMNO’s coalition support.

🎯 PH Callout

You asked us to vote for justice. We voted. You then traded that justice for parliamentary seats. Najib lives in a mansion. Zahid walks free. And the rakyat pays RM50 billion in 1MDB debt. This isn’t justice delayed — it’s justice murdered by the very people who promised to deliver it.

Sources

  • The Star — 1MDB Fiscal Burden
  • CNA — Anwar Faces Graft Crisis

item.title: CPI Score Flatlines at 50 — Same as During Peak 1MDB Scandal in 2015
item.description:
Said by: Transparency International / Rafizi Ramli

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-01-28

📌 What Happened

Malaysia’s Corruption Perceptions Index stuck at 50 points and 57th globally in both 2023 and 2024 — identical to the score during the peak 1MDB scandal in 2015. Rafizi himself warned this is ‘happening despite Anwar’s zero-tolerance stance on corruption.’

🔥 Why It Matters

The CPI is the most widely cited international benchmark for corruption. After two years of Anwar’s ‘zero tolerance’ rhetoric, Malaysia’s score has not moved a single point. The country is perceived as exactly as corrupt as it was when Najib was siphoning billions through 1MDB. This is not a lag effect — the CPI reflects current perceptions based on surveys of business leaders and experts.

💩 Why It’s Bullshit

They are telling the world that despite the change in government, the corruption environment has not improved. When your own minister (Rafizi) publicly cites this score as evidence of failure, the verdict is unanimous.

🎯 PH Callout

Your CPI score is the same as Najib’s during 1MDB. Not similar — identical. After two years of ‘zero tolerance’ speeches, the international community perceives Malaysia as exactly as corrupt as it was under the most kleptocratic government in its history. That’s not a lag — it’s a verdict.

Sources

  • The Vibes — Rafizi on CPI

item.title: Jho Low STILL Free After 8 Years — Malaysia's Most Wanted Fugitive Living Comfortably Somewhere
item.description:
Said by: Interpol / MACC / DOJ

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-01-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: As of 2025, Low Taek Jho (Jho Low) — the central figure in the 1MDB scandal — remains free despite Interpol red notices, international arrest warrants, and charges in multiple jurisdictions. Three different Malaysian governments have failed to secure his extradition. Anwar made 1MDB accountability his signature promise, yet has made no visible progress in locating or extraditing Jho Low. Reports suggest he has been residing in China or Macau, but the Malaysian government has not disclosed any diplomatic efforts to secure his return.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Jho Low orchestrated the theft of billions from a Malaysian sovereign wealth fund. He is the most wanted fugitive in Malaysian history. His freedom — eight years after charges were filed — represents the single largest accountability failure in Malaysian governance. Every day Jho Low remains free is a day the rakyat services debt he helped create while he lives comfortably beyond the reach of Malaysian law. If Malaysia cannot bring its most-wanted kleptocrat to justice, its entire anti-corruption framework is performative.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Anwar spent years demanding 1MDB accountability. “Where is Jho Low?” was a PH rallying cry. Three years into government, the answer remains the same: somewhere comfortable, apparently beyond Anwar’s reach. The US DOJ recovered over $1 billion in 1MDB-linked assets. Singapore jailed bankers. Switzerland sanctioned institutions. Malaysia — the country that was robbed — has not managed to bring the principal orchestrator to trial. If the political will existed, diplomatic pressure on China (where Jho Low is believed to reside) would be a priority. It clearly isn’t.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Under Najib, the 1MDB investigation was actively suppressed — the AG was fired, MACC officers were transferred, and Jho Low was protected. Under Muhyiddin, investigations continued but Jho Low remained free. Under Anwar, investigations concluded with settlements and Najib’s reduced sentence, but Jho Low’s extradition has not progressed. The pattern suggests that every Malaysian government has found it politically convenient to talk about catching Jho Low while doing nothing that would actually bring him back.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: “Where is Jho Low?” — you asked that question for five years. Now you’re the PM. You control the diplomatic apparatus. You have Interpol red notices. You have international cooperation frameworks. And Jho Low is still free. Disclose what diplomatic steps you’ve taken. Publish the extradition requests. Pressure China publicly if necessary. Or admit that catching Jho Low was always a slogan, never a priority — and that the biggest kleptocrat in Malaysian history will die free because no government cared enough to bring him home.

Sources: Reuters | DOJ | SCMP

item.title: Sabah and Sarawak Autonomy Demands Still Unmet — East Malaysia Remains the Forgotten Stepchild
item.description:
Said by: Sabah & Sarawak Leaders / GPS

Category: Politics

Date: 2025-01-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: Despite 2021 constitutional amendments restoring Sabah and Sarawak’s status as equal partners in the Federation, East Malaysia remains structurally marginalised. Sabah is Malaysia’s poorest state. MA63 (Malaysia Agreement 1963) recommendations are gathering dust. Oil and gas royalty disputes remain unresolved. Equal partnership exists on paper but not in budget allocation, infrastructure investment, or political representation. Sarawak’s GPS maintains leverage through coalition politics, but Sabah — where PH won only 1 of 20 seats in the 2025 state election — has almost no bargaining power.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Sabah and Sarawak contributed the resources — oil, gas, timber, land — that built Malaysia’s modern economy. In return, they received the worst infrastructure, healthcare, education, and connectivity in the country. The MA63 framework guarantees equal treatment that has never been delivered. When Sabah remains the poorest state despite sitting on some of Malaysia’s richest natural resources, the federal system has failed East Malaysia. Every year the disparities persist is another year the federation’s promise is broken.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Anwar promised to honour MA63 and treat Sabah and Sarawak as equal partners. Three years later, the specific demands — increased oil royalties, control over immigration, education autonomy, infrastructure investment — remain unresolved. The constitutional amendments were symbolic victories that changed no budget line items. Sabah still has the highest poverty rate. Sarawak still has the worst road infrastructure. Both still have the weakest healthcare coverage. Symbolic equal partnership without fiscal equal partnership is not partnership — it is a rebrand.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Under BN, East Malaysian states were coalition partners who received patronage in exchange for parliamentary seats — but never structural reform. Under PH 1.0, a committee was formed to study MA63. Under PH 2.0, the committee’s recommendations remain largely unimplemented. Under every government, the pattern is the same: promise East Malaysian autonomy, study the issue, delay implementation, and rely on coalition arithmetic to avoid accountability. Sixty years after Malaysia Agreement, the agreement’s terms remain unfulfilled.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You amended the Constitution to restore equal partner status. Then you failed to follow through with equal budgets, equal infrastructure, and equal services. Sabah is still the poorest state. Sarawak still has the worst roads. Both still have the worst healthcare access. Constitutional amendments without fiscal commitments are empty gestures. Increase federal allocation to East Malaysia proportional to their resource contribution. Resolve oil royalty disputes. Deliver infrastructure on par with Peninsular Malaysia. Or keep calling them “equal partners” while treating them like subsidiaries.

Sources: Malaysiakini | Borneo Post | East Asia Forum

item.title: MCA to PH: 'Don't Blame BN for YOUR Failure to Reform'
item.description:
Said by: Neow Choo Seong, MCA Youth

Category: Politics

Date: 2024-12-30

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In late December 2024, MCA Youth spokesman Neow Choo Seong issued a statement telling PH to stop blaming BN for PH’s failure to implement promised reforms. Neow argued that “power is firmly in PH’s hands” and BN should not be scapegoated for PH’s own inaction. The statement was notable because MCA is a BN component party within the unity government coalition — meaning even PH’s own coalition partners were tired of PH blaming everyone else for its failure to deliver reform.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When your own coalition partner publicly tells you to stop making excuses, the excuse has expired. PH’s default defence for every broken promise has been “coalition constraints” — specifically, UMNO and BN blocking reform. MCA’s statement demolishes that argument from inside the coalition: power is PH’s. The PM is PH. The Finance Minister is PH. The majority of cabinet positions are PH. If reforms haven’t happened, it’s because PH chose not to implement them — not because BN prevented it.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH has used “coalition constraints” as an all-purpose excuse for three years. Can’t repeal the Sedition Act? Coalition constraints. Can’t reform the Printing Presses Act? Coalition constraints. Can’t deliver PM term limits? Coalition constraints. MCA’s statement exposes the fraud: PH controls the government. PH has a parliamentary majority. PH holds the key portfolios. The reforms aren’t blocked — they’re not attempted. Blaming BN is not a political strategy; it’s a confession that PH never intended to deliver the reforms it promised.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Blaming coalition partners for policy failures is a Malaysian political tradition. Under BN, UMNO blamed MCA and MIC for “not understanding Malay sensitivities.” Under PH 1.0, PH blamed Mahathir’s faction for slow reform. Under PH 2.0, PH blames UMNO. The pattern is identical across coalitions: the dominant party uses smaller partners as scapegoats for its own policy failures. When even the scapegoat says “stop blaming us,” the strategy has collapsed.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your own coalition partner is telling you to stop making excuses. Power is in your hands. You hold the PM’s office, the Finance Ministry, and the majority of cabinet seats. If reforms haven’t happened after three years, it’s because you chose not to implement them. Stop blaming BN. Stop blaming “coalition constraints.” Own your record. Deliver the reforms you promised — or admit that the promises were never sincere, and face the voters with that admission.

Sources: Malay Mail | FMT

item.title: Asia Maior: Anwar Sidelines 'Open Discussion on Multiple Lines of Disenfranchisement'
item.description:
Said by: Asia Maior Academic Journal

Category: Politics

Date: 2024-12-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In late 2024, the academic journal Asia Maior published an assessment of Anwar Ibrahim’s governance, concluding that he faced challenges “without substantially interrogating a public discourse that has historically sidelined open discussion on multiple socio-economic lines of disenfranchisement.” The analysis documented how Anwar’s reformist rhetoric coexisted with a status quo reality — maintaining the same patronage structures, ethnic frameworks, and institutional arrangements that he had spent decades promising to dismantle.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When an academic journal assesses that your government avoids “open discussion on multiple lines of disenfranchisement,” it is saying that you have chosen political safety over substantive reform. The lines of disenfranchisement in Malaysia are well-documented: ethnic quotas in education and public sector employment, gender inequality in legal rights, wealth concentration among politically connected elites, and East Malaysian marginalisation. PH promised to address all of these. Asia Maior’s assessment suggests none have been substantively confronted.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Anwar built his political career on challenging the very structures Asia Maior identifies as untouched. Reformasi was supposed to mean reforming the ethnic patronage system, opening public discourse, and creating a Malaysia based on merit rather than identity. Three years into government, the discourse hasn’t opened — it has been managed. The patronage system hasn’t been dismantled — it has been rebranded. PH’s approach to disenfranchisement is identical to BN’s: acknowledge it in speeches, avoid it in policy, and hope the economy grows fast enough that nobody notices.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Every Malaysian academic assessment follows a similar trajectory. International scholars note the gap between PH’s rhetoric and reality with increasing precision. The Rosa Luxemburg Foundation documented “corruption, racial divides, and economic discontent.” East Asia Forum documented reform aspirations clashing with political realities. Asia Maior documented the avoidance of open discourse. Each assessment comes from a different angle but reaches the same conclusion: PH talks reform but practices status quo governance.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Academic journals are documenting what Malaysians already know: your reform rhetoric doesn’t match your governance reality. “Multiple lines of disenfranchisement” remain unaddressed because addressing them would cost you politically. Open the discourse Anwar promised. Commission an independent review of ethnic quotas, public sector hiring, and wealth distribution. Publish the data. Let Malaysians debate the findings. Or keep avoiding the conversation while scholars document your avoidance — and watch the reform mandate evaporate.

Sources: Asia Maior

item.title: Rosa Luxemburg Foundation: 'Corruption, Racial Divides, Economic Discontent' Under Anwar
item.description:
Said by: Rosa Luxemburg Stiftung Analysis

Category: Politics

Date: 2024-12-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In late 2024, the Rosa Luxemburg Stiftung (RLS), a German political foundation, published a detailed assessment of Malaysia under Anwar Ibrahim. The report concluded: “High-level corruption persists, rising racial tensions with politicians exploiting sensitive issues, government response focused on managing incidents through archaic laws rather than promoting open dialogue.” RLS documented how the Anwar government maintained the same patronage structures and ethnic frameworks it had promised to dismantle, while using repressive laws to manage dissent rather than addressing root causes.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When an international political foundation with no partisan stake in Malaysian politics describes your government as maintaining “high-level corruption” and managing racial tensions through “archaic laws,” it is a clinical diagnosis of governance failure. RLS is not an opposition mouthpiece — it is an academic institution that analyses political economies globally. Its assessment aligns with HRW, Amnesty International, Asia Maior, and East Asia Forum — a consensus among international observers that PH’s reform agenda has been abandoned.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH’s defence against every international assessment is the same: “they don’t understand Malaysian context.” But when five different international institutions — from different continents, with different methodologies — all reach the same conclusion, the problem is not their understanding. It is your governance. RLS specifically noted that the government’s response is to manage incidents rather than promote open dialogue. That’s not reform — it’s crowd control. Managing racial tensions through the Sedition Act is not addressing racism. It is suppressing the conversation while the underlying problems fester.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: International assessments of Malaysian governance have followed a consistent arc under PH 2.0. HRW: “Reform Agenda Stalled” (2024), “Rights Backslide” (2025). Amnesty: “Betrayed reform pledges” (2024). RSF: 34-place drop in press freedom (2024). Asia Maior: avoids “open discussion on multiple lines of disenfranchisement.” East Asia Forum: “reform aspirations clashed with political realities.” RLS: “corruption, racial divides, economic discontent.” Each assessment arrives independently at the same destination: PH talks reform but governs like BN.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: International observers have reached a consensus: your reform agenda has been abandoned. Corruption persists. Racial tensions are managed through archaic laws, not addressed through open dialogue. Economic discontent is growing. You can dismiss one critical report. You cannot dismiss five from five different institutions on five different continents. Either recommit to the reform agenda that got you elected — or accept that the international community has classified your government as what it is: a continuation of the status quo with better English.

Sources: Rosa Luxemburg Stiftung

item.title: Anwar's Overseas Trips: 75% Funded by Private Companies — Then Government Says Actually No
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister (and Fahmi Fadzil)

Category: Politics

Date: 2024-11-22

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In November 2024, Anwar told Parliament that private companies like Petronas and Proton covered approximately 75% of his overseas travel costs under a new cost-sharing strategy. The next day, Communications Minister Fahmi Fadzil “clarified” that corporate executives “paid their own way” while the government covered Anwar’s delegation. The government had spent RM1.6 million to charter a plane for a four-country trip. When questioned further, Anwar called it a “crazy era” that transparency was being punished. CNA analysts warned the practice “could expose vulnerabilities in Malaysia’s political financing framework.”

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: The PM said one thing in Parliament — private companies covered 75% of travel costs. His Communications Minister said something different the next day — companies paid for their own executives, not Anwar’s delegation. Which is it? If private companies are co-funding PM travel, there are legitimate questions about access and influence. Companies that fly with the PM get face time, relationship-building, and perceived government endorsement. That access has value — and when it’s purchased through travel funding, it raises conflict-of-interest concerns.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Calling critics ungrateful for questioning government spending is not transparency — it is deflection. Anwar styled the cost-sharing as fiscal responsibility, but the messaging collapse within 24 hours suggests the arrangement was either poorly structured or poorly understood by his own cabinet. A government that can’t keep its story straight on travel expenses for one news cycle is not demonstrating transparency — it is demonstrating dysfunction. And RM1.6 million to charter a plane for four countries is not exactly belt-tightening, regardless of who pays.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: PM travel expenses have been contentious under every administration. Najib’s use of a government jet for personal trips and the purchase of a luxury jet were signature scandals. PH criticised every ringgit of Najib’s travel spending. Under PH 2.0, Anwar has taken over 100 overseas trips in three years — more than any previous Malaysian PM in the same period. The cost-sharing arrangement was supposed to answer criticism about travel frequency. Instead, it created a new controversy about corporate influence.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Your PM told Parliament one thing. Your Communications Minister said something different the next day. Then your PM complained that transparency is being punished. That’s not transparency — that’s contradictory messaging followed by self-pity. Publish a clear, detailed accounting of every overseas trip: government cost, corporate participation, and what business outcomes resulted. If the cost-sharing is legitimate, prove it with data. If it’s not, stop doing it. But don’t tell Parliament one figure and have your minister contradict it within 24 hours — and expect Malaysians to call that governance.

Sources: CNA | Malay Mail | FMT

item.title: MACC Seizes RM6M and Freezes Assets Linked to Selangor Menteri Besar Inc in Sand Mining Corruption Probe
item.description:
Said by: Amirudin Shari / MACC

Category: Politics

Date: 2024-10-18

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In October 2024, the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) seized RM6 million and froze assets linked to Selangor Menteri Besar Incorporated (MBI) in connection with a sand mining corruption probe. MACC Chief Commissioner Azam Baki confirmed that five firms linked to a Selangor politician were under investigation for alleged corruption involving a sand mining concession. The probe uncovered a network of contracts and payments that raised questions about the integrity of Selangor’s sand mining approvals.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Selangor MBI is the state’s investment arm — directly under the Menteri Besar’s purview. When MACC seizes millions linked to the MB’s own corporate vehicle, it implicates the governance of the state’s highest office. Sand mining is one of Selangor’s most corruption-prone sectors, involving environmental destruction, river pollution, and billions in revenue. The fact that MACC had to intervene means internal checks failed.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Amirudin has been MB since 2018. Sand mining corruption in Selangor has been documented for years. Environmental groups have warned about illegal mining destroying riverbanks and poisoning water sources. Under Amirudin’s watch, the problem grew until MACC had to step in. That’s not governance — that’s negligence. The MB either didn’t know (incompetence) or didn’t act (complicity). Neither is acceptable.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: In September 2025, MACC confirmed new developments in the MBI corruption probe. A former Selangor GLC officer was charged over RM1.7 million in graft. Earlier, activist Chegubard lodged a MACC report alleging RM10 billion in irregularities linked to the Selangor Maritime Gateway project. The pattern is consistent: Selangor under PH is a corruption hotspot that the government refuses to acknowledge.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: MACC is investigating your state investment arm for corruption. Your own GLC officers have been charged. Your sand mining sector is a mess. And your response is silence. If this happened under a BN menteri besar, PKR would be holding nightly ceramah demanding resignation. Under Amirudin? Crickets.

Sources: Malay Mail | Yahoo News

item.title: Forest City Casino Saga — Anwar Threatens JAIL for Those Who Report on It
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2024-05-01

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In May 2024, Anwar Ibrahim called allegations about a casino at Forest City in Johor “malicious” and suggested those spreading the rumours could face jail. SCMP reported that Anwar and tycoons connected to the Forest City Special Financial Zone were “urged to walk back threats” after the aggressive response drew backlash. Chegubard — the activist who reported on the casino plan — was actually charged with sedition for his social media post. The government’s response to journalism about Forest City was not transparency or denial with evidence — it was threats of imprisonment.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When a Prime Minister threatens jail for people who report on a development project, it sends a devastating signal about press freedom and public accountability. Forest City is a politically sensitive project — a Chinese-backed development in Johor that the government is promoting as a Special Financial Zone with 0% tax incentives. Questions about what activities will be permitted there are legitimate public interest journalism, not sedition. The threat of jail for reporting is not a response to misinformation — it is a suppression of scrutiny.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: If the casino allegations were false, the government had every tool available to debunk them: issue a formal denial, publish the SFZ regulations, show that gaming is excluded. Instead, Anwar chose threats. That’s not the response of a government with nothing to hide — it’s the response of a government that wants questions to stop. SCMP’s reporting — that Anwar and tycoons were “urged to walk back threats” — suggests even allies recognised the response was disproportionate. When your reaction to journalism is more aggressive than your reaction to corruption, your priorities are transparent.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysian PMs have a long history of threatening critics instead of addressing allegations. Mahathir threatened journalists who questioned his policies. Najib’s government used the Official Secrets Act, Sedition Act, and MACC against critics of 1MDB. Muhyiddin used emergency ordinances to suppress dissent during COVID. PH promised to break this pattern. Instead, Anwar’s response to Forest City reporting was indistinguishable from his predecessors: threaten jail, charge the critic, and hope the story dies.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: The correct response to false allegations is evidence, not threats. If there’s no casino planned at Forest City, publish the SFZ regulations and prove it. If there is, own it and defend the policy. But threatening jail for reporters asking questions about a publicly funded special zone is authoritarian — and you know it, because you spent 25 years on the receiving end of exactly this tactic. Stop threatening journalists. Start answering questions.

Sources: SCMP | Malaysiakini

item.title: Police Crack Down on Bersih Rally Under PH Government — The Reformists Become the Repressors
item.description:
Said by: PH Government; Police (PDRM)

Category: Politics

Date: 2024-02-26

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 26-27 February 2024, Bersih held a rally calling on the PH government to implement the political reforms it had promised during the 2022 election campaign — including institutional independence, anti-corruption measures, and parliamentary oversight. Police responded with a crackdown that drew immediate condemnation from civil society. Lawyers for Liberty issued a statement condemning the government’s response, criticising the police’s lack of understanding of the Peaceful Assembly Act and the Federal Constitution. Bersih also handed a memorandum to the government urging implementation of long-delayed reforms.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Bersih’s rallies were the oxygen of PH’s rise to power. The Bersih 2.0, 3.0, 4.0, and 5.0 rallies between 2011 and 2016 built the mass movement that eventually toppled BN. PH leaders didn’t just support these rallies — they marched in them. Anwar himself was a beneficiary of Bersih’s advocacy. Now, the same government that rode Bersih’s wave to power is using police to suppress Bersih’s demands for the reforms PH promised. Freedom of assembly is only acceptable when it serves PH’s interests.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH leaders spent a decade demanding the right to peaceful assembly. Nik Nazmi — who was literally charged under the PAA for organising a protest in 2013 — was a PH minister when police cracked down on Bersih. LFL’s condemnation specifically cited a 2014 Court of Appeal ruling from Nik Nazmi’s own case that struck down restrictive provisions of the PAA. PH’s own legal victory against protest suppression is being ignored by PH’s own government. This isn’t about public order — it’s about a government that promised reform and is now using the same repressive tools as its predecessors to avoid being held accountable for broken promises.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Under BN, police crackdowns on Bersih rallies were front-page scandals. Tear gas, water cannons, and mass arrests at Bersih 2.0 and 3.0 were cited by international human rights organisations as evidence of Malaysia’s democratic backsliding. PH used these crackdowns to build their narrative as defenders of democracy. Human Rights Watch’s 2024 World Report stated that Malaysia’s new government “largely failed to uphold its pledges to address repression” in its first year — specifically noting the gap between PH’s reform promises and its actual governance record.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You marched with Bersih. You were tear-gassed with Bersih. You used Bersih’s rallies to build the movement that brought you to power. Now you’re sending police after Bersih because they’re demanding you keep the promises you made. The reformists have become the repressors. If you can’t handle peaceful criticism from the very people who put you in power, you don’t deserve that power.

Sources: FMT | The Star | HRW

item.title: Najib's Jail Sentence Halved — Pardons Board Under Anwar's Watch
item.description:
Said by: Pardons Board / King of Malaysia

Category: Politics

Date: 2024-02-02

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 2 February 2024, the Pardons Board — which operates under the constitutional authority of the King, with the PM as a member — halved Najib Razak’s 12-year jail sentence to 6 years for his role in the 1MDB scandal. Najib’s RM210 million fine was also reduced to RM50 million. The decision came amid a “royal addendum” controversy, with Najib’s camp claiming an additional document existed granting house arrest. Reuters reported the pardon came “amid accusations of backsliding on reforms.” Critics called it a backroom deal to keep UMNO in the coalition — UMNO had made Najib’s release a precondition for continued support of Anwar’s government.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Najib was convicted of the largest financial scandal in Malaysian history — billions stolen from a sovereign wealth fund, laundered through global banks, spent on yachts, jewellery, and Hollywood films. The conviction was PH’s signature achievement under PH 1.0. Halving the sentence under PH 2.0 sent a devastating message: in Malaysia, corruption penalties are negotiable if you have enough political leverage. The pardon was not based on new evidence or legal merit — it was based on coalition arithmetic. UMNO’s support for PH came with an implicit price, and the rakyat is watching that price get paid.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Anwar spent his entire political career demanding accountability for Najib. “Malu Apa Bossku” was the slogan PH mocked relentlessly. Now Najib’s sentence is halved, his fine reduced by 76%, and his supporters are pushing for house arrest — all under Anwar’s watch. The Pardons Board includes the PM. The decision cannot be separated from the political context: UMNO wanted Najib’s sentence reduced, UMNO is essential to PH’s majority, and the sentence was reduced. Calling this anything other than a political deal requires a level of naivety that no Malaysian possesses.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Malaysia has a history of politically convenient pardons. Anwar himself was pardoned by the King after PH 1.0’s victory in 2018 — a pardon that was celebrated as justice. The difference: Anwar’s conviction was widely seen as politically motivated. Najib’s conviction was upheld by every level of the judiciary based on overwhelming evidence. Pardoning a political prisoner and reducing the sentence of a convicted kleptocrat are fundamentally different acts — but PH’s willingness to facilitate both suggests that the pardons process is a political tool, not a justice mechanism.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You built your movement on holding Najib accountable. Then you halved his sentence to keep UMNO happy. The rakyat marched in Bersih rallies, voted in GE14 and GE15, and waited years for justice — and you traded it for coalition stability. If Najib’s crime was worth 12 years when PH was in opposition, it doesn’t become worth 6 years just because UMNO is in your coalition. Every ringgit Najib stole is still missing. Every year removed from his sentence is a discount on accountability. The rakyat will remember.

Sources: Reuters | Straits Times | Al Jazeera

item.title: Amnesty International 2024: Anwar 'Betrayed the Reform Pledges He Made to Malaysian Voters'
item.description:
Said by: Amnesty International / Shayna Bauchner

Category: Politics

Date: 2024-01-11

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: In January 2024, Amnesty International’s annual report stated that Anwar Ibrahim had “betrayed the reform pledges he made to Malaysian voters so he could hold his coalition together.” The report, authored by researcher Shayna Bauchner, identified seven areas where Malaysia fell short of its human rights obligations, including freedom of expression, freedom of assembly, media freedom, migrant rights, LGBTQ+ rights, and the death penalty. Amnesty noted that the government had not only failed to advance reforms but had actively used repressive laws it had promised to repeal.

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: When Amnesty International — the world’s largest human rights organisation — uses the word “betrayed,” it is not making a casual criticism. It is documenting a government that explicitly promised specific reforms, received votes on that basis, and then reversed course. The word choice matters: “stalled” implies delay; “betrayed” implies intent. Amnesty is saying that PH’s abandonment of reform was not due to constraints or complexity — it was a deliberate choice to prioritise coalition stability over human rights commitments.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH’s defence — “coalition constraints require compromise” — is exactly the argument BN used for decades. UMNO said it couldn’t reform because of coalition partners. PAS said it couldn’t moderate because of its base. Now PH says it can’t deliver rights because UMNO is in the coalition. The excuse is identical; only the party name has changed. If you knew UMNO would block reforms when you formed the coalition, then your manifesto promises were knowingly false. If you didn’t anticipate it, you’re incompetent. Either way, Amnesty’s use of “betrayed” is accurate.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Amnesty has documented Malaysian human rights failures across every administration. Under Mahathir 1.0, the focus was ISA detentions and crackdowns on dissent. Under Najib, it was 1MDB-related repression and the Sedition Act. Under PH 1.0, Amnesty noted partial progress before the Sheraton Move. Under PH 2.0, the verdict is the harshest yet — because the gap between promise and delivery has never been wider. PH made the most ambitious human rights commitments in Malaysian history, and broke them more comprehensively than any predecessor.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: Amnesty International says you “betrayed” your voters. Not “disappointed.” Not “fell short.” Betrayed. That word will follow this government into every international forum, every diplomatic meeting, and every future election. You can still change the trajectory: repeal the Sedition Act, protect assembly rights, guarantee press freedom, and stop using repressive laws against critics. Or let “betrayed” become the one-word summary of the Anwar era — because right now, Amnesty and HRW agree that it is.

Sources: Amnesty International | Malaysiakini

item.title: Anwar Appoints Own Daughter Nurul Izzah as Senior Economic Adviser — Then Calls It Not Nepotism
item.description:
Said by: Anwar Ibrahim, Prime Minister

Category: Politics

Date: 2023-01-31

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: On 31 January 2023 — two months into his premiership — Anwar Ibrahim appointed his daughter Nurul Izzah Anwar as Senior Adviser on Economics and Finance in the Prime Minister’s Department. This came after Nurul Izzah lost her Permatang Pauh parliamentary seat in GE15 to Perikatan Nasional by nearly 6,000 votes — a seat that was a traditional PKR stronghold. The appointment was unpaid, which Anwar used as his primary defence. He told reporters: “Nepotism is where a family member is given a position to abuse power, enrich themselves, obtain contracts and get paid a huge sum. This is not the case.”

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Nepotism is not defined by salary — it is defined by access. An unpaid adviser to the Prime Minister has more institutional influence than most paid civil servants. The role grants proximity to policy, contracts, and decision-making at the highest level. Appointing a candidate who was just rejected by voters to an unelected advisory role is a slap in the face of democratic outcomes. If the PM can appoint family members to unelected advisory roles and call it acceptable, then every future PM can do the same. The precedent is set.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: Anwar spent 25 years branding himself as the antidote to Malaysia’s entrenched patronage politics. Two months into office, he appointed his own daughter. The defence — “it’s unpaid, therefore not nepotism” — is a textbook deflection. Was there an open call? A shortlist? A vetting committee? No. She was appointed because she is the PM’s daughter, and every Malaysian knows it. Nurul Izzah holds degrees from Johns Hopkins, but qualification is not the issue — process is. Mahathir appointed family members to business positions. Najib’s circle enriched relatives through 1MDB-adjacent deals. Anwar promised to be different. He wasn’t.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Political dynasties are endemic in Malaysian politics. Mahathir’s sons held business empires built on political connections. Najib’s stepson Riza Aziz was charged in the 1MDB scandal. UMNO’s patronage networks enriched families for decades. PH criticised all of this relentlessly. Anwar’s appointment of Nurul Izzah — within weeks of taking office — signalled that the reformasi playbook had an asterisk: does not apply to the PM’s family. Nurul Izzah has since been installed as PKR deputy president, consolidating the Anwar dynasty within the party.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You spent 25 years preaching against patronage. You appointed your daughter within 60 days. She lost her seat and you gave her an advisory role. She’s now PKR deputy president. This is not meritocracy — it’s dynasty-building with better PR. All PM advisory appointments should go through a transparent vetting process. Family members of the sitting PM should be automatically disqualified from unelected roles in the PMO. Acknowledge the optics instead of redefining nepotism to suit your situation.

Sources: SCMP | Straits Times | CNA

item.title: PH's GE15 Manifesto: 60 Promises Made, Majority Broken or Abandoned — The Biggest Con Job in Malaysian Politics
item.description:
Said by: Malaysiakini Manifesto Tracker / Straits Times Analysis

Category: Politics

Date: 2022-11-19

📌 WHAT HAPPENED: PH’s GE15 manifesto — released on 19 November 2022 — contained approximately 60 measurable promises. As tracked by Malaysiakini’s Manifesto Tracker and analysed by the Straits Times, the majority have been broken, delayed, or quietly abandoned. Key failures include: repeal of repressive laws (Sedition Act expanded instead, 90+ arrests), abolition of tolls (government pays RM591M to freeze hikes instead), civil service reform (headcount grew), PM term limits (bill failed in Parliament), ICCPR ratification (not done), UUCA abolition (not done), AG-PP separation (sent to committee), and anti-hopping law strengthening (weakened by exceptions).

🔥 WHY IT MATTERS: Election manifestos are the contract between a government and voters. When a majority of promises are broken, the social contract collapses. PH did not lose these promises to external circumstances — they chose to abandon them. The Sedition Act wasn’t repealed because PH found it useful. Tolls weren’t abolished because concessionaires have political connections. The PM term limit bill failed because PH’s own MPs didn’t show up. These aren’t broken promises — they’re policy reversals dressed up as pragmatism.

💩 WHY IT’S BULLSHIT: PH’s defence is always “coalition constraints” and “we need UMNO’s support.” But PH wrote the manifesto knowing it would need coalition partners. If you promise reforms you can’t deliver because of your own coalition arithmetic, you were lying at the point of the promise, not at the point of the betrayal. The manifesto wasn’t aspirational — it was transactional. PH traded promises for votes, collected the votes, and defaulted on the promises. In any other context, this is fraud.

📜 PREVIOUS CASES: Every Malaysian government has broken manifesto promises. BN’s manifestos were wish lists that no one expected to be honoured. PH 1.0 (2018-2020) achieved partial reforms in 22 months before the Sheraton Move. But PH 2.0 is unique: it has a larger majority, more time, and fewer excuses — yet its reform record is worse than PH 1.0’s. The Straits Times noted that PH’s manifesto compliance rate has declined with each year in power, not improved. Promises aren’t being delayed — they’re being abandoned.

🎯 PH CALLOUT: You wrote 60 promises. You broke most of them. You had three years, a supermajority, and the most favourable parliamentary arithmetic in Malaysian history. If you couldn’t deliver reform under these conditions, you never will. Publish a clause-by-clause manifesto compliance report. Explain which promises were abandoned and why. Set binding timelines for remaining commitments. Or admit what every Malaysian already knows: the manifesto was a campaign prop, not a governing plan.

Sources: Malaysiakini Manifesto Tracker | Straits Times
